perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will not disclose and will keep confidential the information r e- ceived, except to its employees, representatives or ag ents who need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Receiving Party will inform them about the confidential quality of the informati on provided and will ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Receiving Party w ill be responsible for ensuring that the oblig a- tions of confidentiality and non -use contained herein will be strictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents .
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will not disclose and will keep confidential the information received, except to its employees, representatives, or agents who might benefit from access to the Confidential Information. The Receiving Party will generally inform them about the confidential quality of the information provided and attempt to obtain their agreement to keep it confidential. The Receiving Party will endeavor to ensure the obligations of confidentiality and non-use are generally observed and will consider potential liability for the actions of its personnel representatives or agents.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces several ambiguities. Replacing 'need to have access' with 'might benefit from access' broadens the scope of permissible disclosures vaguely. Replacing 'will inform' with 'will generally inform' and 'ensure that their agreement is obtained' with 'attempt to obtain their agreement' weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality among employees. Replacing 'will be responsible for ensuring that the obligations...will be strictly observed' with 'will endeavor to ensure the obligations...are generally observed' creates a less stringent, less enforceable obligation. Replacing 'assume full liability' with 'consider potential liability' further reduces the force of the Receiving Party's responsibility.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the intent and requirements of the Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S. Code § 1836), which aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. The changes weaken the obligation to maintain strict confidentiality, making it more difficult to prove that the Receiving Party took reasonable measures to protect the Disclosing Party's trade secrets, which is a key element in a misappropriation claim under the Act. The 'endeavor to ensure' and 'consider potential liability' phrases fail to establish a clear and enforceable obligation to prevent unauthorized disclosure, potentially undermining trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially undermining trade secret protection, which contradicts the intent of the Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S. Code § 1836), as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The original contract ensured confidentiality strictly, while the modified contract introduces ambiguities making it less enforceable. The phrase 'endeavor to ensure' and 'consider potential liability' weakens the original obligations, and fails to establish a clear and enforceable obligation to prevent unauthorized disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 1 describes civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, reinforcing the importance of protecting trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall treat all Confidential In formation with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties should generally treat all Confidential Information with a degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly weakens the standard of care required for protecting confidential information. Replacing 'shall' with 'should' makes the obligation advisory rather than mandatory. Removing 'the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information' eliminates a clear benchmark for assessing compliance. 'A degree of care' is exceptionally vague and could be interpreted as a very low level of protection, potentially defeating the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the principles of trade secret protection under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to a vague 'degree of care' and removing the comparison to the party's own confidential information, the modified text makes it difficult to argue that the Receiving Party took reasonable measures to protect the Disclosing Party's trade secrets. This undermines the protection afforded by the UTSA, as it sets a standard of care that is too low to adequately safeguard confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the standard of care for protecting confidential information. The original text mandates a specific standard of care ('the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information'), while the changed text introduces ambiguity and discretion ('should generally treat...with a degree of care'). The Law Explanation supports that this undermines the protection afforded by the UTSA, as it sets a standard of care that is too low to adequately safeguard confidential information, making it difficult to argue that the Receiving Party took reasonable measures to protect the Disclosing Party's trade secrets. This aligns with the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition, where a vague legal statement leads to multiple interpretations, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law. There are no law snippets to reference.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall promptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored) , including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed un der this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Disclosing Party may r equest it to the Receiving Party .
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will endeavor to return or destroy most copies (in readily accessible form) of the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Disclosing Party may reasonably request it to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. 'Shall promptly' becomes 'will endeavor to', making the action less mandatory and more discretionary. 'All copies' becomes 'most copies,' introducing ambiguity about which copies must be returned or destroyed. 'In whatever form reproduced or stored' is replaced with 'in readily accessible form', implying that copies stored in less accessible locations (e.g., archives, backup tapes) need not be returned or destroyed. 'May request it' becomes 'may reasonably request it', giving the Receiving Party grounds to dispute the reasonableness of a request and thus avoid complying.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could contradict Article 17 of the GDPR, the 'right to be forgotten' or right to erasure. If the confidential information contains personal data of EU citizens, the modified clause weakens the obligation to completely erase the data upon request or termination of the agreement. The terms 'endeavor', 'most copies', and 'readily accessible form' creates loopholes that could allow the Receiving Party to retain personal data indefinitely, conflicting with the GDPR's requirement for data minimization and the right to erasure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the GDPR's 'right to be forgotten' (Art. 17) as it weakens the obligation to erase personal data. The original text mandates the return or destruction of 'all copies,' while the changed text only requires 'most copies' in 'readily accessible form.' This directly opposes the GDPR's requirement for complete erasure, as the snippet indicates that the 'data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay'. By creating exceptions, the modified text introduces ambiguities, violating the right to erasure by allowing the Receiving Party to retain data in less accessible forms or claim they only need to delete 'most' copies. This is a legal contradiction because it weakens obligations stated in law.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party may retain such of its documents as required to comply with mandatory law, provided that such Confidential Information or copies thereof shall be subject to an indefinite confidentiality obligation.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party may retain some documents as potentially required to comply with certain laws, provided that they attempt to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the exception allowing retention of confidential information. Replacing 'such of its documents as required to comply with mandatory law' with 'some documents as potentially required to comply with certain laws' introduces vagueness about what documents can be retained and under what circumstances. 'Certain laws' lacks specificity, making it difficult to determine whether the retention is actually justified. Removing 'an indefinite confidentiality obligation' and replacing it with 'they attempt to maintain confidentiality' dilutes the duty of confidentiality to a mere attempt, with no clear duration or standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly unrelated, this change could create a conflict with the spirit of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX), specifically Section 802, which addresses the destruction or alteration of documents with the intent to impede or obstruct any investigation. If the Receiving Party retains documents under the vague exception of 'potentially required to comply with certain laws' and only makes an 'attempt to maintain confidentiality', it could raise concerns if those documents are later found to be relevant to an investigation and were not properly protected or disclosed. The ambiguity could be exploited to argue that the retention and handling of the documents was not done in good faith or with the necessary level of diligence to comply with legal and regulatory obligations, including those related to SOX. While SOX doesn't explicitly mandate specific document retention for NDAs, its principles require transparency and good faith in handling potentially relevant documents.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 73 / § 1519 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 1519 : Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 73-OBSTRUCTION OF JUSTICE Jump To: Source Credit §1519. Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy Whoever knowingly alters, destroys, mutilates, conceals, covers up, falsifies, or makes a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matter within the jurisdiction of any department or agency of the United States or any case filed under title 11, or in relation to or contemplation of any such matter or case, shall be fined under this title, imprisoned not more than 20 years, or both. (Added Pub. L. 107–204, title VIII, §802(a), July 30, 2002, 116 Stat. 800 .)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: The Laws That Govern the Securities Industry | Investor.gov Skip to main content The .gov means it’s official. Federal government websites often end in .gov or .mil. Before sharing sensitive information, make sure you’re on a federal government site. The site is secure. The https:// ensures that you are connecting to the official website and that any information you provide is encrypted and transmitted securely. Investor.gov U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission Search Investor.gov The Laws That Govern the Securities Industry Note: Except as otherwise noted, the links to the securities laws below are from Statute Compilations maintained by the Office of the Legislative Counsel, U.S. House of Representatives. These links are provided for the user's convenience and may not reflect all recent amendments. If you have questions concerning the meaning or application of a particular law, please consult with an attorney who specializes in securities law. Securities Act of 1933 Securities Exchange Act of 1934 Trust Indenture Act of 1939 Investment Company Act of 1940 Investment Advisers Act of 1940 Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010 Jumpstart Our Business Startups Act of 2012 Rules and Regulations Securities Act of 1933 Often referred to as the "truth in securities" law, the Securities Act of 1933 has two basic objectives: require that investors receive financial and other significant information concerning securities being offered for public sale; and prohibit deceit, misrepresentations, and other fraud in the sale of securities. See the full text of the Securities Act of 1933 . Purpose of Registration A primary means of accomplishing these goals is the disclosure of important financial information through the registration of securities. This information enables investors, not the government, to make informed judgments about whether to purchase a company's securities. While the SEC requires that the information provided be accurate, it does not guarantee it. Investors who purchase securities and suffer losses have important recovery rights if they can prove that there was incomplete or inaccurate disclosure of important information. The Registration Process In general, securities sold in the U.S. must be registered. The registration forms companies file provide essential facts while minimizing the burden and expense of complying with the law. In general, registration forms call for: a description of the company's properties and business; a description of the security to be offered for sale; information about the management of the company; and financial statements certified by independent accountants. Registration statements and prospectuses become public shortly after filing with the SEC. If filed by U.S. domestic companies, the statements are available on the EDGAR database accessible at www.sec.gov. Registration statements are subject to examination for compliance with disclosure requirements. Not all offerings of securities must be registered with the Commission. Some exemptions from the registration requirement include: private offerings to a limited number of persons or institutions; offerings of limited size; intrastate offerings; and securities of municipal, state, and federal governments. By exempting many small offerings from the registration process, the SEC seeks to foster capital formation by lowering the cost of offering securities to the public. Securities Exchange Act of 1934 With this Act, Congress created the Securities and Exchange Commission. The Act empowers the SEC with broad authority over all aspects of the securities industry. This includes the power to register, regulate, and oversee brokerage firms, transfer agents, and clearing agencies as well as the nation's securities self regulatory organizations (SROs). The various securities exchanges, such as the New York Stock Exchange, the NASDAQ Stock Market, and the Chicago Board of Options are SROs. The
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens confidentiality obligations, potentially conflicting with legal requirements for handling documents in investigations. The original text stated 'such of its documents as required to comply with mandatory law,' establishing a clear link to legal necessity and 'an indefinite confidentiality obligation'. The changed text replaces this with 'some documents as potentially required to comply with certain laws' and 'they attempt to maintain confidentiality'. This vague language introduces significant ambiguity, raising concerns about compliance with laws like the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX), specifically Section 802. SOX 802 addresses the destruction or alteration of documents with the intent to impede or obstruct any investigation. The language in the modified text weakens the obligation to protect documents.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that the Disclosing Party wi ll suffer irreparable damage if its Co n- fidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain injun c- tive relief against a threatened brea ch or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such a breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties acknowledge that the Disclosing Party might experience some damage if its Confidential Information is potentially disclosed in a manner that could be considered a breach of this Agreement, and that the Disclosing Party may seek possible remedies.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly weakens the remedies available to the Disclosing Party in case of a breach. Replacing 'will suffer irreparable damage' with 'might experience some damage' reduces the severity of the potential harm. Replacing 'is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement' with 'is potentially disclosed in a manner that could be considered a breach of this Agreement' introduces ambiguity and weakens the clarity of what constitutes a breach. Replacing 'shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief' with 'may seek possible remedies' makes the availability of injunctive relief uncertain and replaces a definite right with a vague possibility. Replacing 'an award of actual and exemplary damages' with 'possible remedies' eliminates the specific types of damages that could be awarded, leaving the Disclosing Party with unclear and potentially inadequate recourse.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Economic Espionage Act (EEA) primarily deals with criminal penalties for trade secret theft, it also implies the importance of strong civil remedies to deter such activity. By weakening the contractual remedies available to the Disclosing Party, the modification undermines the deterrent effect of both the NDA and the EEA. If the Disclosing Party cannot effectively protect its trade secrets through civil litigation due to the weakened remedies clause, it may be less likely to share information, hindering innovation and potentially impacting economic competitiveness, which the EEA aims to promote by preventing trade secret theft and espionage.

(b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the remedies available to the Disclosing Party, potentially undermining the deterrent effect of the EEA as described in the Law Explanation. The original text ensures the Disclosing Party 'shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief' and 'an award of actual and exemplary damages,' while the changed text only states the party 'may seek possible remedies.' This introduces ambiguity, making it difficult to enforce, as the law explanation states that weakening the contractual remedies available undermines the deterrent effect. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 U.S. Code § 1836, discusses 'appropriate injunctive relief' and 'civil seizure,' indicating that these remedies are contemplated as part of trade secret protection. By weakening the Disclosing Party's right to these remedies, the modified text creates a legal contradiction by potentially making it difficult to enforce under national law.
